does: This paper introduces CLINB, a benchmark for evaluating large language models on climate science question-answering tasks using real user queries and rubrics designed by climate scientists. The benchmark assesses models on multimodal, open-ended questions requiring both knowledge synthesis and evidential grounding. The authors evaluate frontier models using a model-based evaluation process and discover a striking tension: while models demonstrate PhD-level knowledge synthesis and outperform hybrid answers created by experts using weaker models, they simultaneously exhibit severe hallucination problems with references and image citations, indicating poor grounding despite strong substantive knowledge.
connects: This work builds on growing efforts to benchmark specialized domain knowledge in LLMs beyond generic benchmarks like MMLU or HELM. It responds to prior research identifying hallucination and attribution problems in LLMs, particularly for knowledge-intensive tasks requiring factual grounding. The paper extends multimodal evaluation frameworks and incorporates human-in-the-loop evaluation methodologies from domain-specific benchmarking literature. It connects to broader work on evaluating knowledge quality in foundation models and acknowledges the tension between emergent capabilities in knowledge synthesis and persistent failures in verifiable attribution that prior studies have noted.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into resolving the knowledge-grounding gap in LLMs, establishing climate science as a high-stakes domain for testing attribution and hallucination mitigation techniques. The work points toward developing methods that preserve knowledge synthesis capabilities while enforcing rigorous evidential grounding, potentially through improved retrieval-augmented generation, citation mechanisms, or training approaches. CLINB can serve as a testing ground for techniques addressing hallucination in specialized domains and supports the broader agenda of deploying LLMs responsibly in contexts where incorrect attribution and false citations carry real consequences for scientific communication and policy.
